questions.text: For a continuous random variable X with density f_X(x), what does f_X(x) represent?
choices: A density: probability over an interval is the area under f_X between its endpoints
The exact probability that X equals x
The maximum value X can take
questions.explanation: For continuous random variables, P(X = x) is exactly zero for any single point; f_X(x) is a density whose integral over an interval gives the probability of X landing in that interval.

questions.text: Two random variables A and B satisfy P(A|B) = P(A). What does this mean?
choices: A and B are independent — knowing B changes nothing about A's distribution
A and B are perfectly correlated
A and B cannot both be measured at the same time
questions.explanation: Independence is exactly the statement that conditioning on one variable leaves the other's distribution unchanged: P(A|B) = P(A).

questions.text: A discrete random variable takes values 1, 2, 3 with P(X=1)=0.3, P(X=2)=0.4, P(X=3)=p. What is p?
questions.explanation: Probabilities in a valid pmf sum to 1: p = 1 − 0.3 − 0.4 = 0.3.

questions.text: A random variable has E[X] = 5 and E[X^2] = 34. What is Var(X)?
Which statement about variance is correct?
choices: Var(X) is always ≥ 0, since it's an expectation of a squared quantity
Var(X) can be negative if X swings below its mean often enough
questions.explanation: Variance is E[(X − E[X])²], the expectation of a squared (hence non-negative) quantity, so it can never be negative.

questions.text: Two variables have E[AB] = 10, E[A] = 2, E[B] = 4. What is Cov(A,B)?
If Cov(X,Y) = 0 (X and Y are uncorrelated), can you conclude X and Y are independent?
choices: No — zero covariance only rules out a linear relationship; a nonlinear dependence can still exist
Yes — zero covariance always means independence
Only if X and Y are both discrete
questions.explanation: Covariance measures only linear co-movement. Independence is stronger and implies zero covariance, but the converse fails — a classic counterexample is Y = X² for a symmetric X centered at zero.

questions.text: Two variables have Cov(A,B) = 8, Var(A) = 16, Var(B) = 4. What is the correlation coefficient ρ_AB?
questions.explanation: ρ_AB = Cov(A,B) / √(Var(A)·Var(B)) = 8 / √(16×4) = 8/8 = 1 — a perfect linear relationship.

questions.text: One word: Cov(X, X), the covariance of a random variable with itself, is exactly the random variable's ___.
questions.explanation: Cov(X,X) = E[(X−E[X])²] = Var(X) — variance is the special case of covariance where both variables are the same one.
